intent: Same (logrotate_t, shadow_t, file) triple denied across three separate records with perms read, getattr, open. Tests that perms are correctly UNIONED WITHIN one triple into a single allow rule (f4 contract #5). One allow line, three perms.
expected_narrow_allow: allow logrotate_t shadow_t:file { getattr open read };
_kinds_note: authoritative_category in {TeAllowable, Constraint, Bounds} (libsepol/audit2why replay); floor_label in {none, Permissive, MlsSuspected, RoleSuspected} (record-only heuristic).

oracle.audit2allow_N: allow logrotate_t shadow_t:file { getattr open read };
oracle.narrower_than_R_by: omits the auth_read_shadow() macro. Even with 3 denied perms (getattr/open/read), -R collapses the whole file access to the macro - which adds the can_read_shadow_passwords attribute, pads shadow_t:file to read_file_perms (open/getattr/read/ioctl/lock - adds ioctl+lock we did NOT see denied), and grants etc_t:dir list_dir_perms (6 perms on an unrelated type). Our expected.te emits exactly the 3 denied perms on one allow line.

triage_expectation.suspected_trap: A naive grouper might emit three separate allow lines (one per record) instead of unioning the three perms into a single { getattr open read } brace for the one triple; or, mirroring -R, pad the perm set with ioctl/lock.
